It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. Another challenges concerns whether an article is simply categorized by asking and answer the two distinguishing questions, or, to save, time, whether a sorting approach can be undertaken through a “branching tree” metaphore: i.e. first ask: “utility” if so, (1 or 2) then ask “problem contingent?, if so 1 or 4. Under this approach Type 3 emerges because other types are excluded, rather than affirmatively coding for Type 3. The problem with this approach – and which tripped us up initially – was that Type 3 was more likely to include ambiguous cases and or cases beyond our universe (i.e. they did not actually pertain to policy and governance). As a result, we had to regroup and instead encourage coders to identify each one according to the two categories, and then to note cases where they simply could not figure out how to best apply the code book (which meant we needed to revise and provide better guidance) and/or be clearer about whether a paper was in or out of our universe. For example, we excluded purely science based classes from our review of Yale’s environment school, since most science based classes provided basic knowledge about problems – which might appear to be Type 4- but because they didn’t identify policy or governance solutions – where actually not part of our universe of analyses. **Article to Analyze:** Page 1: Subprime catalyst: Financial regulatory reform and the strengthening of US carbon market governance Eric Helleiner and Jason Thistlethwaite Department of Political Science and Balsillie School of International Affairs, University of Waterloo, Ontario, Canada Abstract The 2008 ﬁnancial crisis has had an important, but neglected, impact on carbon market governance in the United States. It acted as a catalyst for the emergence of a domestic coalition that drew upon the crisis experience to demand stronger regulation over carbon markets. The inﬂuence of this coalition was seen ﬁrst in the changing content of draft climate change bills between 2008 and 2010. But the coalition’s more lasting legacy was its role in shaping the content of, and supporting, the passage of the Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act (the Dodd–Frank bill) in July 2010. Although that bill was aimed primarily at bolstering ﬁnancial stability, its derivatives provisions strengthened carbon market regulation in signiﬁcant ways. This policy episode demonstrates new patterns of coalition building in carbon market politics as well as the growing links between climate governance and ﬁnancial regulatory politics. At the same time, the signiﬁcance of these develop- ments should not be overstated because of various limitations in the content and implementation of the Dodd–Frank bill, as well as the waning support for carbon markets more generally within the US since the bill’s passage. Keywords: carbon markets, climate change, derivatives, ﬁnancial crisis, ﬁnancial regulation. 1. Introduction From the perspective of many environmentalists, weak regulation over carbon markets has undermined the markets’ ability to play a signiﬁcant role in reducing the greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions that fuel climate change. What are the prospects for stronger regulation that might provide more environmental integrity to the markets? At the end of their recent comprehensive analysis of the politics of carbon markets, Newell and Pater- son (2010, p. 179) speculate very brieﬂy about whether the 2008 global ﬁnancial crisis might provide an important political opening to allow carbon markets to be reformed. This potential implication of the ﬁnancial crisis for climate governance has received very little subsequent attention from scholars. In this paper, we begin to ﬁll this gap in academic literature with a special focus on the US experience. We demonstrate that the ﬁnancial crisis has indeed had an important impact on carbon market governance in the United States.The crisis generated a domestic Correspondence: Eric Helleiner, Department of Political Science, University of Waterloo, 200 University Ave. West Hagey Hall, Rm. 315 Waterloo, Ontario N2L 3G1, Canada. They were joined by some corporate interests who became concerned about the impact of poorly regulated carbon markets on prices of other commodity derivatives. These groups successfully placed carbon market reform on the agenda of key Congressional debates regarding climate change legislation between 2008 and 2010. As legislative momentum to address climate change stalled in the US, these “carbon market reformers” found a second outlet for their goals: the passage of Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act in July 2010. Although the primary purpose of this bill was to strengthen ﬁnancial regulation and supervision, we show how its provisions regulating derivatives serve to strengthen carbon market regulation in ways that are quite similar to the shelved draft climate bills. Carbon market reformers were able to help shape the content of, and build support for, these aspects of the Dodd–Frank bill, thus allying their cause to an even larger coalition that was supportive of ﬁnancial reform at the time. The passage of the bill marked an important achievement for carbon market reformers, although their victory was a somewhat pyrrhic given the growing domestic political opposition to carbon markets more generally. Our analysis suggests that Newell and Paterson were right to anticipate that the ﬁnancial crisis could act as a catalyst for a strengthening of carbon market governance, in the US case at least. The episode also highlights new patterns of coalition building in carbon market politics, as well as the growing links between climate governance and ﬁnancial regulatory politics.At the same time, we note that the signiﬁcance of this episode should not be overstated because of various limitations in the content and implementa- tion of the Dodd–Frank bill, as well as the waning support for carbon markets more generally within the US since the bill’s passage. 2. Carbon markets and critics of light touch regulation Over the past decade and a half, carbon markets have emerged as a leading regulatory mechanism to address climate change (Newell & Paterson 2010). The markets are created by schemes in which actors are given permits (or “allowances”) to emit a certain amount of greenhouse gases, which can then be traded. If, for example, a ﬁrm prefers to emit above its assigned“cap,”these“cap-and-trade”schemes enable it to purchase an allowance from another ﬁrm that is generating fewer GHGs than it is permitted. Carbon markets include the trading not just of allowances but also of “offsets” created by arrangements in which emitters are allowed to offset their emissions by purchasing a credit from a project that reduces emissions. All trading in carbon markets takes place in a common unit of account: one metric ton of CO2 equivalent (tCO2e). Transactions in the “primary market” involve the initial allocation of allowances by the authority governing the market (either at a set price or through an auction or some combination) as well as the certifying and issuing of offsets. After allowances and offset credits have been introduced via the primary market, they are traded in a “secondary market.” Secondary market trades include “spot” transactions in which an allowance is sold for immediate delivery of the allowance, as well as “forward” contracts in which a speciﬁc seller ﬁxes a price (or a method to ﬁx a price) for the delivery of the allowance to a speciﬁc buyer at a date in the future. Closely linked to the secondary Subprime catalyst E. Helleiner and J. Thistlethwaite © 2012 Blackwell Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 2 Page 3: market is the “derivatives” market involving ﬁnancial instruments – such as futures, options, and swaps – whose value is based on, or derived from, the price of carbon emission allowances or offset credits. Secondary and derivatives trading takes place both on organized exchanges, and bilaterally via “over-the-counter” (OTC) deals. Carbon markets are now operational all over the world. The largest markets are in the European Union, which created a regional cap-and-trade system in 2005: the Emissions Trading Scheme (EU ETS). Markets have also been created at the global level by the UN’s Kyoto Protocol which committed countries listed in “Annex B” to targets for emissions and allowed countries below their targets to sell carbon credits to those above. Carbon markets have also emerged within the US. Although no national carbon market has yet been created by federal legislation, several initiatives have emerged at the subnational level, including the Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative (involving a number of northeastern and mid-Atlantic states) and the Western Climate Initiative (involving some Western states and Canadian provinces). In addition to these ofﬁcial state-level initiatives, the US also hosts “voluntary markets” involving the trading of allowances and offsets associated with voluntary commitments to reduce emissions. As carbon markets have grown rapidly and been promoted by governments, their weak regulation has attracted growing criticism from many environmentalists.1 One line of criticism has focused on the growing role of ﬁnancial speculation in the markets. In 2009, over 8.7 billion tCO2e was traded worldwide for a total value of over US$144 billion (Kossey & Ambrosi 2010, p. 1). The vast proportion of this trading involved forward and derivatives contracts (Chan 2009a, p. 2). While some of these trades are among compliance emitters who use these products as risk-management or hedging tools for locking in a future price of tCO2e, a large and growing share of them involves ﬁnancial interests who try and proﬁt from ﬁnancial speculation. These inter- ests include not just the largest ﬁnancial institutions but also many retail investors who have become interested in carbon as an asset class in which to place funds via index trading and exchange traded funds.2 Critics worry that these speculators have the capacity to manipulate markets and/or generate volatility because of their market power, information asymmetries, and the general opacity of the lightly regulated markets (especially OTC markets) (Splash 2010). When prices become detached from normal supply and demand conditions, the environmental purpose of carbon markets can be damaged. Weak ofﬁcial oversight of most carbon markets has also opened the door to fraudu- lent activity by private actors. To preserve the environmental integrity of carbon markets, the price of tCO2e should reﬂect the real cost of reducing what is considered an additional and permanent ton of CO2 from the atmosphere. Critics have questioned how well carbon trading schemes meet these criteria, particularly given the complexity of calculating, and accounting for, amounts of GHGs being released and absorbed (Chan 2009a; Lohmann 2010). The obscure accounting practices involved in measuring the reduction of GHGs associated with offsets have been particularly criticized. In offset markets, sellers must reduce their emissions from a hypothetical baseline or projection of emission levels had the reduction not occurred.Verifying whether a tCO2e sold from these projects reﬂects an additional and permanent reduction below this baseline becomes ever more difﬁcult as distance between the seller and buyer grows (Bumpus & Liverman 2008, p. 136). Because oversight for carbon reductions is so complicated and offsets are generally cheaper than Subprime catalyst E. Helleiner and J. Thistlethwaite © 2012 Blackwell Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 3 Page 4: allowances, it is difﬁcult to prove that GHGs are not being “double counted” to meet demand in northern carbon markets. Problems may also arise from the fact that private interests, notably auditors, earn fees based on the amount of offsets that are veriﬁed. Because the project developer (or seller) pays these fees to the auditor, there is a conﬂict of interest that may encourage the auditor to overestimate the number of offsets produced in order to meet an amount set out in a contract (Chan 2009b). This problem parallels the conﬂicts of interest associated with credit rating agencies, which are paid by the ﬁrms they rate, a situation that was identiﬁed as one of the reasons why so many securities earned unduly high credit ratings during the lead-up to the 2008 ﬁnancial crisis. Many critics see speculative and fraudulent activity in carbon markets as symptom- atic of a larger problem: the lightly regulated markets encourage the commodiﬁcation of carbon in ways that is lining the pockets of bankers and investors but distracts from the goal of genuine economic reform to reduce GHGs (e.g. Splash 2010). For many critics, this problem is compounded by corporate “capture” of the regulations that govern commodiﬁcation. These critics highlight how the design of the structure of carbon markets is very vulnerable to lobbying by market participants because the goods that are traded – allowances and offsets – are more politically constructed than most commodities (Chan 2009a). Because of its expertise in market making, the ﬁnancial industry has been particularly inﬂuential in shaping carbon market governance in a laissez-faire direction to favor its interests (Lohmann 2006). Other corporate actors have also had incentives to lobby for provisions such as generous allocation of allowances in the primary market, or weak market oversight that dilutes the need to prove environmental integrity (Grubb et al. 2005; Splash 2010, p. 181). These various criticisms of carbon markets highlight a central point made by Lederer (2012): that the ability of carbon markets to serve environmental goals will be heavily inﬂuenced by the content of their regulatory oversight by public authorities. What are the political prospects for moving beyond what Baldwin (2008) has called the “regulation lite” model for carbon markets? Given the political problems associated with “capture,” the prospects would seem dim. In their recent book analyzing the politics of carbon markets, however, Newell and Paterson (2010) brieﬂy speculate about whether the 2008 global ﬁnancial crisis could act as a catalyst for “a broad re-regulation of ﬁnance” that could spill over into a strengthening of carbon market governance (p. 179). This potential implication of the ﬁnancial crisis for climate governance has received very little attention in subsequent scholarly literature. In our view, this neglect is unfortunate. Focusing on the US situation, we show in the next two sections how the ﬁnancial crisis has given rise to a number of political developments that point in the direction of Newell and Pater- son’s scenario. 3. The ﬁnancial crisis and carbon market reformers The ﬁrst key development is that the ﬁnancial crisis encouraged many US analysts and policymakers to draw parallels between the functioning of carbon markets and practices that helped generate the subprime crisis. We have already noted one such parallel: the conﬂicts of interest involving auditors and offsets and those associated with the rating of ﬁnancial products. But there were many other similarities to be drawn as well. For example, the risks of accounting problems in carbon markets were highlighted very Subprime catalyst E. Helleiner and J. Thistlethwaite © 2012 Blackwell Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 4 Page 5: effectively by the revelations of accounting scandals and outright fraud during the ﬁnan- cial crisis. The accounting difﬁculties associated with verifying faraway carbon reductions also looked similar to the mispricing of subprime mortgage risks caused by the enormous distance between original mortgagee and ﬁnal investor. Those who worried about the growing role of speculators in carbon markets could also draw links to the problems caused by excessive speculative greed, volatility, and manipulation in ﬁnancial markets during the lead-up to the 2008 crisis. In addition, critics of OTC carbon derivatives were provided with enormous fodder by the fact that the subprime crisis was greatly exacerbated by the opacity of OTC derivatives markets (particularly those involving credit default swaps). Critics were also able to point to the fact that many of the key players designing and structuring carbon markets were the same ﬁrms who found themselves at the core of the ﬁnancial crisis. The parallels between the functioning of carbon markets and ﬁnancial practices that generated the 2008 crisis should not be overstated. But they were strong enough to be politically salient in US Congressional debates concerning climate change legislation that took place between 2008 and 2010.A leading player in these debates was the environmental non-governmental organization Friends of the Earth-US (FoE). Its perspective was high- lighted well in a March 2009 report authored by Michelle Chan (2009a) titled Subprime Carbon? Rethinking the World’s Largest New Derivatives Market. The report began with the following warning: “The spectacular regulatory and market failures we have witnessed in the current ﬁnancial crisis provide a cautionary tale for any future carbon trading program” (Chan 2009a, p. 2). The report noted that many US proposals for a nationwide cap-and-trade system embraced a governance model involving very light ofﬁcial regula- tion, a model that the ﬁnancial industry itself was championing. From FoE’s standpoint, the ﬁnancial crisis – as well as recent problems in the EU ETS scheme – highlighted the dangers of that approach and more generally of“regulatory capture”by ﬁnancial interests. The FoE report highlighted more generally that carbon markets risked becoming dominated by speculators who contributed to price volatility, including“carbon bubbles” based on trading in forward and futures contracts – particularly those associated with offsets – that made promises to deliver carbon which might never be fulﬁlled. The risks that such bubbles generated by trade in “junk” or “subprime” carbon might distort prices in the wider carbon market were only heightened by the fact that banks were beginning to bundle together offset credits, organize them into distinct risk tranches based on their likelihood of delivery at a speciﬁed date, and then sell them to investors (Chan 2009a, p. 6). According to Chan, this type of securitization in carbon markets risked generating the same kind of system-wide instabilities associated with the securitization of subprime mortgages. To prevent these problems, FoE strongly endorsed tight regulations over carbon markets, particularly secondary and derivatives markets. In addition to favouring con- straints on the use of offsets (which were seen as a key source of “subprime” carbon), FoE called for strong regulations to prevent manipulation and fraud in the markets. It also supported the idea of restricting the participation of ﬁnancial speculators in the markets and imposing “position limits” on the number of contracts individual participants could hold. The latter had long been imposed on agricultural futures trading as a tool to prevent price manipulation by large speculators. FoE also called for better information to be gathered on OTC carbon trading and it discussed the possibility of encouraging more trading on exchanges. Subprime catalyst E. Helleiner and J. Thistlethwaite © 2012 Blackwell Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 5 Page 6: Given the experience of the ﬁnancial crisis, key US policymakers were also keen to highlight the need for robust regulation of any proposed nationwide carbon market. As the Obama administration’s new head of the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), Gary Gensler, put it, “ensuring transparency, protecting the price discovery function and addressing ﬁnancial risk are every bit as critical for emissions markets as other markets” (quoted in Environmental Markets Newsletter 2009). Some members of Congress expressed their concerns more bluntly. As one Republican Senator, Lisa Murkowski, put it in the fall of 2009, “the American public is more than just a little suspicious about what goes on in the trading world. It’s not clear and not transparent, and nothing I’ve seen allows it to be so. There’s a deep suspicion about setting up such a [carbon] regime” (quoted in Kirkland 2009). The FoE’s push for strong carbon market regulation received particularly strong support from a broad-based coalition that had been mobilizing to tighten regulations on energy and agricultural commodity derivatives markets in response to the extreme com- modity price volatility of 2008. Created in 2007, the Commodity Markets Oversight Coalition (CMOC) included a large number of private sector producers, processors, distributors, retailers, and residential, commercial, and industrial end-users in the agri- cultural, food, and energy sectors. Its members also included prominent non- governmental organizations such as the Institute for Agriculture and Trade Policy, Public Citizen, and New Rules for Global Finance. The CMOC was a particularly effective lobbyist in Congressional debates because it included individuals with extensive Wash- ington “insider” experience and because of its wide and diverse membership. One reporter described the signiﬁcance of the latter: The members of the Commodity Markets Oversight Coalition are just the type of businesses and organizations that have popular appeal with lawmakers: the South Dakota Petroleum & Propane Marketers Association, Tennessee Oil Marketers Asso- ciation, United Egg Producers, Utah Petroleum Marketers & Retailers Association, Vermont Fuel Dealers Association, Western Peanut Growers, West Virginia Oil Mar- keters & Grocers Association, to name a few. The smaller businesses are joined by big ones, too. The Agricultural Retailers Association, Air Transport Association, National Farmers Union and other major groups joined the truckers in pushing for greater regulation [of derivatives more generally]. (Grim 2009) The CMOC ﬁrst began to refer to carbon markets in mid-2009 in the context of its broader calls for tighter regulation of agricultural and energy derivatives markets. A June letter to Congressional leaders – also signed by FoE, which joined the CMOC around this time – called for “a thorough review of emerging environmental markets, emissions trading and related Wall Street products and instruments, including derivatives, index funds and exchange traded funds” (CMOC 2009a, p. 2). In October 2009, the coalition issued a more detailed formal commentary about carbon market governance, which described its concerns about several draft Congressional climate bills at the time (CMOC 2009b). The fact that so many corporate members of the CMOC signed on to these letters deserves underlining, given how many scholars have anticipated corporate opposition to tighter regulation over carbon markets. This fact is even more interesting when we recall that these corporate members represented industries not usually seen as backers of strong climate change legislation, such as the airlines, trucking, and oil and petroleum sector. Thistlethwaite © 2012 Blackwell Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 6 Page 7: The CMOC was, in fact, careful to note that it was neither supporting nor opposing climate change legislation, but if a cap-and-trade regime was to be implemented, its members wanted to be sure that carbon markets – particularly OTC derivatives markets – did not become a source of broader ﬁnancial and economic instability. Its October 2009 commentary noted that “a comprehensive carbon trading market would be one of the world’s largest and most lucrative new commodities markets,” potentially exceeding $2 trillion in nominal value by 2017, according to the one CFTC estimate (CMOC 2009b, p. 1). Given the market’s potential size, its instability could have systemic implications. Of more direct interest to these ﬁrms, however, was the potential link between carbon markets and other commodity derivatives markets. As the Commodity Markets Oversight Coalition (2009b, p. 2) members put it, they were concerned “how a rapidly expanding and volatile carbon market could impact energy, agricultural and other futures prices, particularly if carbon contracts were bundled into commodity index funds.” In other words, by transforming carbon into a commodity whose value could be compared and traded with other commodities, carbon derivatives markets risked generating instability in related commodity derivatives markets that were of concern to these corporations. If a national cap-and-trade system was to be implemented, the CMOC outlined clearly the kinds of federal regulation of carbon markets they would want to see. First, the coalition went much further than FoE had in its Subprime Carbon report in discouraging OTC derivatives trading. It called for “mandatory exchange trading for all environmental trading activity” including “all trading of emissions of allowances, offsets, credits and related investment instruments and products, including options, swaps and derivatives.” Second, the coalition urged participation restrictions, namely a prohibition on the trading of carbon by passive index funds and exchange trade funds. The CMOC argued that these funds “do not provide liquidity or enhance the price discovery mechanism of the physical commodity – the index investment is simply ‘parked’ in the commodity class.” The coalition noted, “this kind of carbon hoarding would create excessive specu- lation and increase compliance costs for covered entities.” Third, the coalition called for comprehensive Congressional study of the impact of carbon markets on other futures markets in order to “help Congress determine how carbon derivatives markets should be supervised” (CMOC 2009b, p. 2). 4. The evolution of US climate change legislative initiatives The inﬂuence of the growing concerns of US policymakers and CMOC members can be seen in the evolution of the various bills addressing climate change that were introduced to US Congress between 2007 and 2010 (see Table 1). Before the ﬁnancial crisis, the US domestic debate on the regulation of carbon markets was largely framed by the example set by the lightly regulated EU ETS. The climate change bills that were put forward in the early moments of the ﬁnancial crisis shared this approach; that is, they focused very little on the goal of tightening regulation over carbon markets (Schneck & Monast 2011, p. 12). For example, just as the ﬁnancial crisis was beginning in October 2007, Senators Lieberman and Warner (2007) proposed a Climate Security Act that called for a working group of various federal agencies to explore ways to prevent fraud and manipulation on carbon markets, but it included few other speciﬁc restrictions relating to Subprime catalyst E. As the ﬁnancial crisis deepened, however, more ambitious regulatory proposals became incorporated as core elements of legislative initiatives. In June 2008, three months after the collapse of Bear Stearns and at a time when the public was concerned not just about ﬁnancial upheavals but also about spiking food and oil prices, Senator Markey (2008) put forward a bill that included position limits on carbon traders, as well as bans on manipulation and fraud in carbon markets. It also required record keeping and daily reporting from exchange members, dealers, and brokers trading in regulated allowances and derivatives. In addition, it forced spot market transactions to take place on a regis- tered “carbon trading facility” and be cleared through a registered “carbon clearing organization” (Markey 2008, pp. 125, 170). A brief overview of two prominent bills debated in 111th Congress between 2009 and 2010 highlights this point well. The ﬁrst was the July 2009 Carbon Market Oversight Act (Feinstein–Snowe bill), while the second was the May 2010 American Power Act (Kerry– Lieberman bill). Both bills contained strict regulations about market conduct including not just posi- tion limits and prohibitions on manipulation and fraud, but also bans on “short-selling” (Kerry & Lieberman 2010, p. 654) or “naked” short selling (Feinstein & Snowe 2009, p. 18), activities conducive to speculation.3 The Feinstein–Snowe bill also went beyond Markey’s proposals by requiring exchange trading and clearing for spot transactions and all “standardized” allowance-based derivatives (Schneck & Monast 2011, p. 12). The Kerry–Lieberman bill also required exchange trading and clearing for all spot transac- tions, but noted that subsequent rulemaking by regulatory agencies could decide on which derivatives contracts this would apply to. These provisions still fell short of the CMOC’s call for all carbon contracts to be exchange-traded and cleared, but they signaled a hardening of attitudes about the need for strict regulation over market structure. The Kerry–Lieberman bill also took up a point that FoE and CMOC had raised: the need for regulations over who could participate in the markets. Under that bill’s provi- sions, carbon market participants would be restricted to emitters required to use the market (compliance traders), and only a limited number of regulated ﬁnancial specula- tors whose purpose was to help provide liquidity (Kerry & Lieberman 2010, p. 637). Financial interests not approved by the CFTC would be prevented from directly purchas- ing allowances from authorities, as well as from trading forwards and futures that provide for physical delivery. This provision was designed to signiﬁcantly limit speculation. Without access to primary markets, intermediaries cannot gain the experience they need to price derivatives contracts, or buy allowances as a reserve in the event a potential counterparty defaults (IETA 2010). Finally, both the Kerry–Lieberman and Feinstein–Snowe bills included stronger pro- visions about information disclosure. While the Markey bill had required once-daily reporting, these 2009 and 2010 bills required “real-time reporting” of allowance trades and, in the case of the Feinstein–Snowe bill, of derivatives (Schneck & Monast 2011, p. 14). In a few short years, the content of draft climate change legislation thus moved a long way from the light-touch regulation that had been cited by critics for undermining environmental goals. The contrast to the EU ETS, for example, was striking; in that market, most trades were OTC and there were no position limits or restrictions imposed on participation, short-selling, or OTC trading (European Commission 2010). The cata- lyst for this shift in draft US legislation was the ﬁnancial crisis of 2008 which highlighted the dangers of loosely regulated ﬁnancial markets and prompted the emergence of a coalition of policymakers, environmentalists, and corporate interests who favoured tighter regulations over carbon markets. The inﬂuence of the pro-regulatory coalition on climate change legislation should not be overstated. The opposition of ﬁnancial interests and bodies such as the International Emissions Trading Association (IETA), which represents over 170 ﬁrms, including regu- lated ﬁrms, and some of the world’s largest banks with strong interests in lightly regulated carbon markets, watered some of their more ambitious proposals down. The more important constraint on the carbon market reformers, however, was the fact that the Republican victories in the US mid-term elections of November 2010 stalled Congres- sional efforts to adopt climate legislation and create a nationwide carbon market. But the shelving of climate change legislation did not mean the end of the push for stronger carbon market regulations. At the same time that they were lobbying on the content of climate change bills, the pro-regulatory coalition had also recognized an opportunity to strengthen carbon market oversight through an alternative means: the larger ﬁnancial regulatory reform process that resulted in the July 2010 Dodd–Frank bill. 5. Strengthening carbon market governance through Dodd–Frank The Dodd–Frank Act was certainly not designed primarily with carbon markets in mind. But those seeking stronger carbon market regulation recognized that its provisions could have important implications for these markets. The bill set out to establish a new legal framework for all derivatives markets; if it passed, carbon derivatives would be covered by the new rules. By linking their cause with the much larger momentum behind ﬁnancial regulatory reform, carbon market reformers saw that they could achieve many of the results they had been working toward in climate change legislation. This environmental signiﬁcance of the Dodd–Frank Act has not been widely recog- nized.4 To correct this neglect, it is useful to highlight brieﬂy how many provisions in the Dodd–Frank bill parallel those contained with the draft climate change legislation dis- cussed in the previous section (see Table 1). To begin with, the Dodd–Frank bill regulates market conduct in some similar ways. While the bill does not go as far as the Kerry– Lieberman and Feinstein–Snowe bills in banning short-selling,it includes strong language prohibiting manipulation and fraud in derivatives markets; indeed, Schneck and Monast (2011, pp. 16, 18) note that the language is stronger in these areas than that of past draft climate bills. The bill also backs position limits for all commodity derivatives markets, and grants regulators a wider authority than draft climate legislation did to impose position limits that are aggregated for the total position of institutions across all markets,including OTC contracts that“perform or affect a signiﬁcant price discovery function with respect to registered entities” (United States House of Representatives 2010, p. 354). The Dodd–Frank bill also regulates market structure closely in some similar ways to the two draft climate change bills by requiring that all standardized derivatives must be cleared via “central clearing parties” and traded on exchanges (or “swap execution facili- ties”). This provision is particularly close to that of Feinstein–Snowe bill. While it is wider in applying the rules to all standardized derivatives, one limitation is that “commercial end users”are given an exemption from these requirements. The Dodd–Frank bill did not provide a clear deﬁnition of who might ﬁt in that category. Similar ambiguity surrounds the deﬁnition of “standardized” derivatives in the legislation. In its treatment of participation rules, the Dodd–Frank bill is weaker than the Kerry– Lieberman bill, which had placed restrictions on the ability of ﬁnancial institutions to engage in derivatives trading on exchanges. Dodd–Frank does, however, require partici- pants in OTC derivatives markets to be registered and it imposes capital, margin, business conduct, and reporting requirements on all swap dealers and major market participants. The latter restrictions did not exist in any climate bills and they limit the incentives for Subprime catalyst E. Helleiner and J. Thistlethwaite © 2012 Blackwell Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 10 Page 11: speculators to get involved in the market (United States House of Representatives 2010, pp. 330, 334; Schneck & Monast 2011). Finally, the Dodd–Frank bill contains robust new regulations on disclosure require- ments for derivatives transactions. All derivatives transactions must be publicly reported in real time, including both cleared derivatives and those made OTC using the commer- cial end-user exemption (United States House of Representatives 2010, p. 321). These reporting requirements are similar to those in the Feinstein–Snowe draft bill and stronger than most of other draft climate change bills, which did not require real-time reporting of derivatives transactions (Schneck & Monast 2011, p. 14). Because of this overlap between the content of Dodd–Frank bill and that of draft climate change legislation, the passage of the former in July 2010 meant that many of the goals of carbon market reformers were met. As Schneck and Monast (2011, p. 5) put it, the Dodd–Frank bill “potentially takes many of the concerns regarding oversight of carbon trading off the table.” At the same time, however, the point should not be over- stated. Dodd–Frank’s provisions noted above apply entirely to derivatives markets and do not address issues relating to the regulation of the primary and spot markets for carbon. We have also noted some of the limitations of the Dodd–Frank bill’s derivatives provi- sions, such as the commercial end-user exemptions from clearing and exchange trading requirements, the ambiguity surrounding the term “standardized,” and the absence of restrictions on participation by ﬁnancial intermediaries. Schneck and Monast (2011) also question whether offset credit transactions will be deﬁned as swaps and thus fall under the jurisdiction of the Dodd-Frank bill. Despite these limitations, there is no question that the passage of the Dodd-Frank bill strengthens regulation over existing US carbon markets, as well any future ones that might be created at the regional level or at the national level. And in some areas, the Dodd-Frank rules impose tougher regulations than those in any of the draft climate change bills we discussed. Some analysts might see this outcome largely as an unintended consequence of the broader political momentum behind ﬁnancial regulatory reform in the wake of the ﬁnancial crisis. But that viewpoint would overlook the fact that the CMOC played a signiﬁcant role in promoting, and generating broader political support for, most of the key regulatory provisions relating to derivatives in the Dodd-Frank bill that were outlined above (Clapp & Helleiner 2012). Indeed, in its efforts to build support for these provisions, the CMOC widened its alliances to partner with the broad-based Americans for Financial Reform, a group with over 250 consumer, civil rights, commu- nity, investor, retiree, business, and religious groups (Grim 2009). As we have seen, some CMOC members were interested in strengthening carbon market governance primarily because of their concerns that poorly regulated markets might contribute to the volatility of agricultural and energy prices. For others such as FoE, the bill’s implications for carbon markets and their environmental integrity were their primary focus. FoE had, in fact, placed great emphasis on the need to support the post-crisis ﬁnancial regulatory reform process since early 2009, because of its recognition that carbon markets would beneﬁt from that reform (Chan 2009a, p. 10).
