Thistlethwaite © 2012 Blackwell Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 11 Page 12: the CFTC. This committee, which had been created in February 2008, was to serve “as a vehicle for discussion and communication on matters of concern to exchanges, ﬁrms, end-users, and regulators regarding energy and environmental markets.” The Commodity Markets Oversight Coalition (2010, p. 7) noted, that “for several energy and consumer groups in CMOC, the EEMAC has been a valuable forum for discussion of the Commission’s oversight role in the energy and environmental markets and has provided an opportunity to address thoughts and concerns directly to Commissioners and their staff.” The other reference to carbon markets was more direct. Section 750 of the Dodd– Frank bill commissioned an interagency working group to conduct “a study on the oversight of existing and prospective carbon markets to ensure efﬁcient, secure and transparent carbon markets, including the oversight of spot markets and derivative markets” (IWG for the Study on Oversight of Carbon Markets 2011, p. 1). This working group provided advocates of carbon market reform with an opportunity to build upon the gains made in the Dodd–Frank regulations and press for further improvements in the areas noted above. After soliciting public input, the interagency working group (IWG) published its study in January 2011. The study was skeptical of some initiatives that reformers had urged, such as stronger participation restrictions. Although it acknowledged that “excessive speculation can lead to market distortions” (IWG for the Study on Oversight of Carbon Markets 2011, p. 23), the IWG suggested that such activity could be regulated effectively through the use of position limits and the monitoring of participants’ position. In other ways, however, the study was very encouraging for groups such as FoE. While noting that the Dodd–Frank bill provided a “comprehensive regulatory oversight program” (IWG for the Study on Oversight of Carbon Markets 2011, p. 50) for carbon derivatives, the IWG argued that there was still much work to be done to address the governance of primary and secondary markets that were not yet subject to the same comprehensive oversight. It highlighted the need to prevent “manipulation, fraud and other market abuses,” as well as to protect both the economic and “environmental integrity” of these markets (IWG for the Study on Oversight of Carbon Markets 2011, pp. 13, 50). Improving oversight of primary and secondary markets was deemed particularly important because of the “interdependence of primary, secondary and derivatives carbon markets” (IWG for the Study on Oversight of Carbon Markets 2011, p. 51). The Dodd–Frank bill thus not only had an immediate impact in tightening regulation over carbon derivatives, but also generated momentum for further strengthening of regulatory oversight of carbon markets in the future. The episode shows how the ﬁnancial crisis changed the politics of carbon markets in the US. In addition to generating new criticism of light-touch regulation among policymakers, societal groups, and even cor- porate interests, the subprime crisis created new links between carbon market politics and broader ﬁnancial regulatory politics. The ability of the carbon market reformers to achieve their goals ultimately rested on the links they made with a much broader coalition within US politics that backed ﬁnancial regulatory reform in the wake of the ﬁnancial crisis. Environmental groups such as FoE cleverly recognized this point and exploited its potential to work toward their environmental goals. These achievements of carbon market reformers should not be overstated. Opposi- tion to the Dodd–Frank bill from some corporate interests – especially in the ﬁnancial sector – succeeded in diluting many of its derivatives provisions before its ﬁnal passage. The role of speciﬁc private interests in contributing to these outcomes demonstrates their enduring inﬂuence over the trajectory of climate change governance. From this perspective, carbon markets may be reformable, but only at the cost of becoming irrelevant. Whether that interpretation of the politics of carbon markets holds water over the medium term remains to be seen. 6. Conclusion Many environmentalists have been skeptical about the use of carbon markets as a leading regulatory mechanism to address climate change. Because of weak ofﬁcial oversight, the markets seem designed more to make money for proﬁt-seeking corporate interests than to reduce GHG emissions. As we have seen, however, the subprime ﬁnancial crisis acted as a catalyst for a substantial strengthening of US carbon market governance by public authorities. It prompted many Americans to draw links between market failures in the ﬁnancial system and potential threats to the integrity of carbon markets stemming from their weak regulation. Out of concern for the latter, a coalition emerged involving poli- cymakers and environmental groups.A number of corporate interests joined the push for tighter regulation because of their worries about the impact of volatile carbon markets on other commodity derivatives markets. This broad-based coalition succeeded in inserting provisions for stronger regulatory oversight of carbon markets into draft federal climate change legislation being debated between 2008 and 2010. As the passage of climate change legislation stalled in 2010, proponents of stricter carbon market regulation found a second channel for their cause in post-crisis legislative initiatives to tighten regulation over ﬁnancial markets. They helped craft, and build political support for, the derivatives provisions of the Dodd–Frank bill whose passage then had the effect of implementing many of the carbon market reforms that draft climate bills had called for. The ﬁnancial crisis thus had precisely the impact on carbon market governance Newell and Paterson speculated it might have: it acted as a catalyst in the US for a “broad re-regulation of ﬁnance” that helped strengthen the governance of carbon markets. This trend was also driven by the kind of “novel and imaginative forms of coalition and alliance-building” that Newell and Paterson (2010, p. 188) suggested might be needed to generate a form of “climate capitalism” that served environmental goals more effectively. The CMOC itself was already a very diverse coalition. In the course of lobbying for the Dodd–Frank bill, its members then reached out even more widely to groups supportive of ﬁnancial regulatory reform more generally. Scholars already describe climate governance Subprime catalyst E. Helleiner and J.
